coredata.dc:title: Bifunctional 0D carbon quantum dot cathode electrocatalyst coupled with atomic hydrogen and hydroxyl radicals for efficient removal of oxytetracycline hydrochloride

coredata.dc:description: Efficient removal of antibiotics is difficult to achieve by conventional single-oxidation or reduction processes. Herein, a bifunctional cathodic electrocatalyst coupling atomic hydrogen (H*) reduction with hydroxyl radical (·OH) oxidation in an efficient electrocatalytic process was developed for the effective degradation of oxytetracycline hydrochloride (OTC). Pd particles were dispersed on B-doped carbon quantum dots to prepare a Pd-B-doped carbon quantum dots/titanium (Pd-BCQD/Ti) electrode as a bifunctional cathode catalyst. The electrode can simultaneously generate H* through the H2O/H+ reduction process, H2O2 through the oxygen reduction process, and then generate ·OH. H* performs nucleophilic hydrogenation reduction of OTC molecules, and ·OH performs electrophilic oxidation of the carbon skeleton, and both act synergistically with the active chlorine species (HClO) generated at the anode. The experimental results showed that the electrode achieved 90.50 % removal of OTC within 60 min. In addition, the Pd-BCQD/Ti electrode reduces energy consumption while increasing current efficiency. The degradation effect was more than 85.80 % in the pH range of 3∼9, indicating fairly stable catalytic activity over a wide pH range. The degradation efficiency was basically unchanged after ten cycles, and the reactivity was still high in the actual water. Intermediates were analyzed based on LC-MS to explore the degradation pathways of OTC. The toxicity of the intermediates generated during OTC degradation was predicted based on quantitative conformational relationships. This study provides a feasible strategy for the preparation of bifunctional catalysts based on zero-dimensional BCQD for efficient green antibiotic wastewater treatment.

head.ce:title: Bifunctional 0D carbon quantum dot cathode electrocatalyst coupled with atomic hydrogen and hydroxyl radicals for efficient removal of oxytetracycline hydrochloride

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Efficient removal of antibiotics is difficult to achieve by conventional single-oxidation or reduction processes. Herein, a bifunctional cathodic electrocatalyst coupling atomic hydrogen (H*) reduction with hydroxyl radical (·OH) oxidation in an efficient electrocatalytic process was developed for the effective degradation of oxytetracycline hydrochloride (OTC). Pd particles were dispersed on B-doped carbon quantum dots to prepare a Pd-B-doped carbon quantum dots/titanium (Pd-BCQD/Ti) electrode as a bifunctional cathode catalyst. The electrode can simultaneously generate H* through the H2O/H+ reduction process, H2O2 through the oxygen reduction process, and then generate ·OH. H* performs nucleophilic hydrogenation reduction of OTC molecules, and ·OH performs electrophilic oxidation of the carbon skeleton, and both act synergistically with the active chlorine species (HClO) generated at the anode. The experimental results showed that the electrode achieved 90.50 % removal of OTC within 60 min. In addition, the Pd-BCQD/Ti electrode reduces energy consumption while increasing current efficiency. The degradation effect was more than 85.80 % in the pH range of 3∼9, indicating fairly stable catalytic activity over a wide pH range. The degradation efficiency was basically unchanged after ten cycles, and the reactivity was still high in the actual water. Intermediates were analyzed based on LC-MS to explore the degradation pathways of OTC. The toxicity of the intermediates generated during OTC degradation was predicted based on quantitative conformational relationships. This study provides a feasible strategy for the preparation of bifunctional catalysts based on zero-dimensional BCQD for efficient green antibiotic wastewater treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Broad-spectrum antibiotics, such as oxytetracycline hydrochloride (OTC), are commonly used in animal healthcare [1,2]. Numerous studies have shown that OTC is frequently found in the aquatic environment [3]. Its complex structure makes it difficult to biodegrade, resulting in harmful effects on the environment [4,5]. Consequently, the rising pollution of the aquatic environment due to oxytetracycline hydrochloride has raised significant public concern [6,7].

Advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) are capable of generating powerful reactive substances (e.g., hydroxyl radical (·OH)) and are promising technologies for degrading antibiotics [8–11]. In the realm of wastewater treatment, electrocatalysis is a popular advanced oxidation technique that has the benefits of easy operation, environmental friendliness, and lack of secondary contamination [12]. Electrocatalysis is capable of generating ·OH (Eq. (1) with strong oxidative capacity at the anode to decompose organic pollutants [13]. In parallel with ·OH generation, Activated hydrogen atoms (H*) (Eqs. (2) and (3) can be generated on the cathode surface [14,15]. Specifically, the cathode can adsorb protons at low overpotentials to generate H* substances with high reducing properties and adsorb them on the electrode surface. The H*-mediated indirect reduction pathway of target pollutants is further realized [16–18].

The selection of electrode materials is pivotal for the efficacy of electrocatalysis [19]. At present, several proposed electrocatalysts, including palladium and its alloys, have been thoroughly investigated through both experimental and theoretical calculations [20]. Palladium (Pd) exhibits a minimal lattice misfit of merely 0.77 %, and its atomic hydrogen adsorption energy closely mirrors that of Platinum (Pt) [21]. The formation of Pd-Hx is facilitated when H+ions occupy the octahedral voids within the Pd lattice [22]. Consequently, Pd-based catalysts demonstrate enhanced efficiency in reducing antibiotics. Furthermore, by maintaining a high level of activity towards the Hydrogen Evolution Reaction (HER), these catalysts also bolster the cathode's resistance to corrosion [22–24]. Yang et al.[25] developed a novel nickel foam electrode (Pd@Ni-foam) adorned with ultrafine palladium nanoparticles for the electrocatalytic degradation of florfenicol. Toxicity evaluations revealed that the bacteriostatic activity of florfenicol was entirely neutralized following electrocatalytic treatment. However, given palladium's scarcity and high cost, enhancing its reactivity and minimizing its usage constitute a judicious strategy [16]. Palladium has proven effective in augmenting the reactivity in composite electrode design techniques such as defect engineering [20], facet engineering [26], coordination chemistry [27], and bimetallic fabrication [28]. When applied to titanium electrodes, Pd acts as an efficient catalytic cathode for degrading organic pollutants. Conversely, Pd particles directly deposited onto Ti electrodes tend to aggregate, leading to diminished activity [29,30]. To address this issue, a conductive intermediate layer comprised of carbonaceous material was employed to offer an optimal structure for the incorporation of Pd particles [31,32]. Ma et al.[33] engineered a Pd/graphene-Nafion/titanium mesh electrode specifically for the degradation of chlorophenols, achieving complete dechlorination within 90 min. The results indicated that the electrocatalytic efficacy of the electrode could be enhanced by incorporating a graphene-Nafion intermediate layer. Employing this strategy allows for the modification of the catalytic electrode's interface, thereby exposing additional active sites.

Zero-dimensional carbon quantum dots (0D CQDs) represent a category of carbon nanomaterials that have gained prominence in fields such as catalysis, sensing, energy conversion, and biomedicine. This popularity stems from their low cost, high electrical conductivity, and the abundance of functional groups on their surface [34]. The nearly uniform distribution of charge and spin densities on the individual atoms of Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) typically renders them inactive in electrocatalytic reactions. This inactivity stems from the lack of active sites capable of facilitating the specific adsorption of reactants like OH–, H+, and O2 [35]. However, it is feasible to transform this inertness into activity by designing and synthesizing XCQD (where X represents a heteroatom, such as B, N, F, S, etc.) through a heteroatom doping strategy. Among the various heteroatoms, Boron (B) atoms are less electronegative than Carbon (C) atoms. The introduction of B atoms leads to the formation of a graphite-like BC3 structure, which is believed to significantly enhance the conductivity and catalytic activity of Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs)[36]. Consequently, Boron-doped CQDs (BCQDs) can serve as an ideal intermediate layer to effectively immobilize Palladium (Pd) particles while providing catalytic activity.

This work reports the successful top-down production of B-doped carbon quantum dots with nanostructural integrity. Next, a Pd-BCQD/Ti bifunctional cathode was created using the BCQD as an intermediary layer to facilitate the electrocatalytic degradation of OTC. This study set out to: (i) systematically assess the bifunctional Pd-BCQD/Ti electrode's catalytic activity for the electrocatalytic degradation of OTC; (ii) explain how ·OH, H*reactive chlorine species and H2O2 form and play important roles in electrocatalysis; (iii) evaluate the electrode's practical applicability in various antibiotic solutions and aqueous matrices; and (iv) investigate the biotoxicity of OTC and its degradation intermediates during electrocatalysis. This study is expected to improve our understanding of the electrocatalytic degradation mechanism of BCQD composite electrodes, which can contribute to the effective remediation of actual polluted water bodies.

Ti flakes with a purity of >99.9 % were purchased from Aoshuo Metal Materials Co. Since all of the chemicals and reagents were analytical grade, no additional purification was needed for the experiments. Ultrapure water was used to prepare all of the solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Pd and BCQD co-modified Ti electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, BCQD were synthesized by a top-down method, see Text S1 in the Supporting Material for details, and BCQD/Ti support electrodes and Pd-BCQD/Ti composite electrodes were prepared by two-time electrodeposition. See Text S2 in the Supporting Material for detailed information. The specific preparation process is shown schematically in Scheme 1 .

Using a three-electrode setup, all electrochemical tests were carried out on a Princeton electrochemical workstation. The working, counter, and reference electrodes were titanium electrode, platinum sheet, and saturated calomel electrode (SCE), in that order. Each Na2SO4 electrolyte solution contained 0.5 mol/L. Plots of electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were made between 105∼0.01 Hz. At a rate of 0.167 mV/s, the Tafel polarization curve was scanned. The scanning potential range used for cyclic voltammetry scans (CV) was −0.6∼1 V. The aim was to characterize the actual active surface area through monitoring. Hydrogen evolution potentials were determined by linear scanning voltammetry (LSV) in a 0.5 mol/L Na2SO4 solution at a scan rate of 50 mV/s. Furthermore, to evaluate the potential for chlorine precipitation and the production of reactive chlorine species, linear voltammetric scans were performed in solutions containing different concentrations of Na2SO4 and NaCl. The platinum electrode served as the working electrode in this instance, the saturated calomel electrode served as the reference electrode, and the titanium electrode served as the counter electrode.

In a single-chamber electrolysis reactor equipped with a heat-absorbing magnetic stirrer, a NaCl solution was used as the supporting electrolyte to electrolyze 100 mL of OTC-simulated effluent. The electrocatalytic degradation behaviors were examined under various conditions: using different cathodes (Ti, CQDs/Ti, BCQD/Ti, Pd/Ti, Pd-CQD/Ti, and Pd-BCQD/Ti electrodes), at different NaCl concentrations, degradation currents, initial pH levels, initial OTC concentrations, and temperatures. Additionally, the effects of differing water qualities and antibiotics were explored. Notably, the prepared electrodes, with an effective area of 4 cm2, and platinum sheets, with an area of 1 cm2, were positioned parallel to each other with a separation of 2 cm, acting as the cathode and anode, respectively. Each experiment was conducted using a DC power supply, with continuous stirring applied at various currents for a duration of 1 h. Samples were collected every 10 min for subsequent analyses. High-Performance Liquid Chromatography-Mass Spectrometry (LC-MS) analysis was employed on these samples to elucidate the degradation mechanism of OTC. Additionally, biotoxicity assessments of over-the-counter medications and their degradation products were performed using the ECOSAR and TEST software tools.

The surface morphology of various electrodes was examined using scanning electron microscopy. Fig. 1 (a) reveals that the Ti substrate's surface is relatively flat, displaying some water waveform patterns, which are not optimal for the subsequent loading of BCQD. In contrast, the etched Ti substrate exhibits a rougher surface and higher porosity, facilitating more attachment sites for the subsequent loading of Pd particles and BCQD, as shown in Fig. 1(b). Following constant current deposition, BCQD covered the electrode surface in cluster compound forms (Fig. 1c), providing more active sites for the reaction due to the catalytic activity of BCQD. Pd particles are evenly distributed across the electrode surface, as observed in Fig. 1(d). The effective synthesis of 0D carbon quantum dots is further evidenced by the TEM image of BCQD presented in Fig. 1(e). The uniform distribution of five elements—C, B, O, Pd, and Ti—on the Pd-BCQD/Ti electrode is confirmed by the elemental distribution map (Fig. 1f). This indicates the successful deposition of Pd particles and BCQD onto the Ti substrate.

Fig. 2 (a) displays the XPS full-scan spectrum of Pd-BCQD/Ti, revealing the coexistence of elements C, O, Pd, and B. The C 1s spectrum in Fig. 2(b) can be deconvolved into four distinct peaks corresponding to C-B (283.9 eV), C=C/C–C (284.6 eV), C-O (285.6 eV), and C=O (288.4 eV) [37,38]. The presence of B doping in BCQD is evidenced by the C-B bonding. According to the high-resolution O 1s spectra in Fig. 2(c), lattice oxygen (OL, 529.9 eV) and surface hydroxyl oxygen (Oad, 531.5 eV) are the primary components of the O atoms. The existence of Oad aids in the oxidation of water molecules to ·OH, thereby enhancing the catalytic activity of the electrodes [39]. The B 1s spectra in Fig. 2(d) feature three characteristic peaks associated with B4C (187.5 eV), BC3 (189.9 eV), and BCO2 (192.6 eV) [40]. To promote the adsorption of O intermediates on the catalyst, B atoms can be appropriately attached to them. Additionally, substituting the sp3 C atom with a B atom to form a BC3 structure leads to charge redistribution and the creation of polarized active catalytic sites [41]. Consequently, the incorporation of B enhances the catalytic activity of CQDs. Fig. 2(e) displays two Pd 3d peaks located at 334.9 eV and 340.1 eV, which correspond to Pd 3d5/2 and Pd 3d3/2, respectively. These results suggest the successful incorporation of Pd particles and highlight the role of Pd0 in catalyzing the degradation of OTC [42]. Fig. 2(f) presents the XRD spectrum of the composite electrode, showing the typical peaks of Ti (101), (102), (103), (112), and (201) at 40.16°, 52.92°, 70.90°, 76.30°, and 77.60°, respectively. The absence of significant characteristic peaks may be attributed to the thin layer of BCQD and the low crystallinity of carbon quantum dots. However, a characteristic peak associated with the Pd (111) crystal plane was detected at a 2θ value of 40.16°, indicating the successful loading of Pd. Notably, there is an overlap between the diffraction peaks of the Pd (111) and Ti (101) crystal faces.

The cyclic voltammograms are displayed in Fig. 3(c). The greater electrochemically active region of the Pd-BCQD/Ti electrode is confirmed by the observation that the area occupied by the CV curves of the electrodes loaded with BCQD and Pd is much bigger than that of the BCQD/Ti and Ti electrodes. Fig. 3d shows the results of the linear scanning voltammetry (LSV) test in the absence of added OTC. It can be seen that the overpotentials of the electrodes of Ti, BCQD/Ti and Pd-BCQD/Ti were −1.46, −1.41 and −1.27 V, respectively. The data showed that the electrode of Pd-BCQD/Ti had the best electrocatalytic performance. When BCQD and Pd particles were added, the hydrogen precipitation potential of the electrode was positively shifted, a result that suggests that it is responsive at smaller potentials [18,43].

The relationship between the concentration of the BCQD deposition solution and OTC degradation efficiency demonstrates an initial increase followed by a decline. According to Fig. 4 (a), when the concentration of the BCQD deposition solution increases from 1 mg/L to 20 mg/L, the OTC degradation efficiencies are 60.33 %, 64.40 %, 69.12 %, 67.21 %, and 60.96 %, respectively. The k-values corresponding to BCQD concentrations ranging from 0 to 20 mg/L are as follows: 0.01546, 0.01745, 0.01974, 0.01856, and 0.01531 min−1 (as shown in the insets of Fig. 4a). While BCQD loading can enhance the catalytic activity of electrodes, an excessive concentration can overload the electrode surface, impeding the mass transfer process during the reaction. Furthermore, such overloading may damage the electrode surface, leading to the shedding of BCQD and a subsequent decrease in OTC removal. Comparatively, the BCQD/Ti electrode prepared with a 10 mg/L deposition solution exhibited superior catalytic performance relative to other concentrations. Hence, a concentration of 10 mg/L was determined to be the optimal BCQD deposition level.

Because Pd particles serve as active sites for the electrocatalytic activity, the amount of Pd loaded onto the electrode surface affects how quickly OTC degrades. By adjusting the PdCl2 concentration in the deposition solution, Pd-BCQD/Ti electrodes with various Pd loadings were created, and OTC deterioration tests were conducted independently. The breakdown efficiencies of Oxytetracycline hydrochloride at concentrations of PdCl2 of 5, 10, 15, 20, and 25 μmol/L, respectively, were 69.83 %, 74.91 %, 70.91 %, 68.44 %, and 66.79 %, as indicated in Fig. 4(b). As the concentration of PdCl2 increased, the clearance rate of OTC first increased before declining. The reduction efficiency of H* on OTC may be lowered by an excessive Pd loading when the concentration of PdCl2 was too high, which would subsequently result in a decrease in the degradation effect. At a PdCl2 concentration of 10 μmol/L, the reaction rate constant k increased to 0.02306 min−1 (as seen in the inset in Fig. 4b). As a result, Pd loading affected the OTC degrading action, and 10 μmol/L was determined to be the optimal PdCl2 concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of degradation effect of different electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The Pd-BCQD/Ti electrodes were found to outperform the Pd/Ti electrodes in terms of electrocatalytic activities and synergistic effects when compared with undoped B carbon quantum dots/titanium supported electrodes (CQDs/Ti) and Pd/Ti electrodes. These electrodes were fabricated using optimal BCQD and PdCl2 concentrations for OTC degradation. The efficiency of OTC degradation increased in the following order: Ti (57.91 %) < Pd/Ti (61.88 %) < CQDs/Ti (61.91 %) < Pd-CQDs/Ti (68.75 %) < BCQD/Ti (69.12 %) < Pd-BCQD/Ti (74.91 %). Incorporating CQDs improved the degradation effect compared to the pure Ti electrode. The degradation effect was further enhanced by 7.21 % when B was doped, surpassing the performance of CQDs/Ti. This indicates that the addition of B atoms to the 0D CQDs structure increases the number of active sites, thereby improving electrochemical behavior and degradation efficiency. Notably, the Pd-BCQD/Ti electrode amplified the degradation impact by 13.03 % compared to the Pd/Ti electrode, demonstrating the catalytic activity of both Pd and B-CQD particles (Fig. 4c). These results align with the findings from the electrochemical performance analysis, highlighting a synergistic effect between Pd and BCQD. Additionally, the OTC degradation by various electrodes followed first-order reaction kinetics. Overall, the composite electrode made of Pd, BCQD, and Ti proved to be an effective electrocatalytic material for OTC breakdown, with the Pd-BCQD/Ti electrode achieving the highest reaction rate constant (k = 0.02306 min−1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of current efficiency and energy consumption of different electrodes
ce:section.ce:para:
The current efficiency is closely related to the current intensity, electrode performance, etc. Improving the current efficiency can promote the generation of ·OH and accelerate the removal of OTC. The current efficiencies (CE) of the electrocatalytic degradation of OTC by Ti substrate and Pd-BCQD/Ti electrode were explored, and then the performances of the two electrodes were compared. The CE for electrocatalytic degradation of 1 g of OTC was calculated as shown in Eq. (4).
(4)

where C0 is the initial concentration of OTC (g/L), Ct is the final concentration of OTC (g/L), F is Faraday's constant (96485C/mol), V is the volume of reaction solution (L), I is the current intensity (A), and t is the reaction time (s).

Calculated by Eq. (4), the current efficiency of Pd-BCQD/Ti electrode for OTC degradation under the same experimental parameters (C0 = 0.02 g/L, V=0.1 L, I=0.005 A, t = 3600 s) is 1.004 %, which is an increase of 0.228 % when compared with that of the Ti electrode (CE=0.776) before modification, and the results indicate that the modified Pd-BCQD/Ti electrode enhanced the current efficiency on the basis of improved OTC degradation.

In addition, electrode energy consumption is an important performance indicator of the electrocatalytic process, and the efficient treatment of difficult-to-degrade organics with the lowest energy consumption is an important research direction of electrocatalytic technology. The energy consumption per stage (EEO) for degrading OTC is calculated as shown in Eq. (5).

where Ecell is the electrolytic cell voltage (V), I is the current (A), t is the treatment time (h), V is the volume of treated water (m3), and C0 and Ct are the initial and final concentrations of OTC (mg/L), respectively.

The electrical energy consumption of the Pd-BCQD/Ti electrode for OTC degradation under the same experimental parameters was calculated by Eq. (5) to be 0.229 kWh/m3, which was reduced by 0.026 kWh/m3 compared with that of the Ti electrode before the modification (EEO=0.255 kWh/m3). The modified Pd-BCQD/Ti electrode in this study improved the OTC removal efficiency while decreasing power consumption and reducing the operating cost.

Fig. 4(d) illustrates the investigation into the influence of electrolyte NaCl concentration on OTC degradation. When the NaCl concentration was below 0.5 mol/L, the degradation efficiency improved from 33.40 % to 82.07 %. This suggests that as the NaCl concentration increased, the efficiency of OTC degradation also rose. The underlying reason is that at lower electrolyte concentrations, an increase in the electrolyte concentration leads to higher solution conductivity and a faster rate of electron transfer. Consequently, the internal resistance of the solution decreases, which, in turn, enhances the effective current density while maintaining a constant applied current strength. The efficiency of OTC elimination improves due to accelerated mass transfer and more frequent collisions between oxytetracycline hydrochloride molecules and electrodes, facilitated by higher electrolyte and ion concentrations in the solution [44]. However, when the electrolyte concentration exceeds 0.5 mol/L, the effectiveness of OTC degradation begins to decline. This decrease is attributed to the elevated inter-ionic forces that occur when the ion concentration is too high, which are detrimental to the degradation reaction [45]. Additionally, a salt film may form on the anode's surface as a consequence of the electric field's activity, leading to the migration of further anions towards the anode. Preventing OTC from contacting the anodic active site can reduce the effectiveness of OTC degradation [44]. It is worth noting that a higher electrolyte concentration results in a lower cell voltage, which can conserve electricity. However, a low voltage may not provide sufficient potential to initiate electron transfer, thereby decreasing OTC removal, consistent with the energy band theory [46]. The optimal NaCl concentration was determined to be 0.5 mol/L, as the reaction rate constant peaked at 0.02532 min−1 when this concentration was achieved (Fig. S1 a).

The current magnitude will have some effect on the electrocatalytic degradation of OTC, as shown in Fig. 4(e), the effect of OTC degradation increases and then decreases when the applied current increases from 0 mA to 11 mA. The removal efficiency was only 5.68 % when the current was 0 mA, indicating a weak adsorption effect of the electrode. The degradation efficiencies of OTC at 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, and 11 mA were 60.36 %, 72.95 %, 82.07 %, 85.77 %, 90.50 %, and 87.77 %, respectively. The degradation constant followed the same trend with the maximum value of k at 9 mA, which was 0.03429 min−1 (Fig. S1 b). This is due to the fact that as the current increases, more active substances can be produced, favoring the degradation of OTC. An excessive current causes a lot of bubbles to form close to the cathode, which strips the active layer that is put onto the electrode surface and reduces the degrading effect. Considering that applying a high current would favor the occurrence of hydrogen evolution side reactions, which would have a competitive effect with OTC degradation and increase the cost, subsequent experiments were performed at an applied current of 9 mA.

Fig. 4(f) shows that the degradation efficiency increases with the initial concentration when the OTC concentration is below 20 mg/L. However, when the OTC concentration exceeds 20 mg/L, the decomposition efficiency decreases. The Pd-BCQD/Ti electrode exhibited optimal degradation performance at 20 mg/L, achieving a maximum degradation rate of 90.50 %. This phenomenon is primarily due to the limited number of active species produced electrochemically in the electrocatalytic degradation system. As the number of OTC molecules per unit volume surpasses the electrode's degradation capacity, some OTC molecules remain undegraded, thereby reducing the degradation efficiency. Moreover, if the initial OTC concentration is too low, there will be an insufficient number of OTC molecules, leading to underutilization of the active species and a diminished degradation effect. Consequently, the initial OTC concentration in this study was set at 20 mg/L to maximize the utilization of the active species generated during the electrochemical process.

Fig. 4(g) illustrates an experiment designed to explore how varying initial pH values influence the degradation rate of oxytetracycline hydrochloride. The degradation efficiencies were observed to be 70.41 %, 86.00 %, 90.50 %, 90.53 %, 90.06 %, 85.80 %, and 76.15 % at initial pH values of 1, 3, 4.5 (initial pH), 5, 7, 9, and 11, respectively. The effectiveness of OTC removal was found to increase initially and then decrease as the pH values rose. The highest degradation efficiency of 90.53 % was achieved at pH 5. A significant drop in degradation rate was noted at extremely low pH values, attributed to the effective layer shedding on the electrode's surface, which led to a decline in degradation efficiency. At high pH values, the inhibition of ·OH generation was observed. Notably, the composite electrode demonstrated remarkable stability in degrading 70.41 % of OTC within 60 min at pH 1, indicating that the Pd-BCQD/Ti electrode maintains stable catalytic activity across a wide pH range (1–11).

The findings of the zero charge point (pHpzc) are displayed in Fig. 4(h). It is evident that the Pd-BCQD/Ti electrode has a pHpzc = 5.47. It is well known that a material's surface is positively charged when it is below pHpzc and negatively charged when it is above pHpzc. The morphological distributions of OTCs vary depending on the pH, with cations (pH≤3.22, H3OTC+), amphoteric ions (pH=3.22∼7.46, H2OTC±), anions (pH=7.46∼10.00, HOTC−), and bi-anions (pH>10.00, OTC2−) being the most common [47]. Therefore, because of electrostatic attraction, the removal effectiveness of OTC is high in the appropriate pH range, which is 3.22∼7.46. It indicates that the electrode has a greater catalytic impact throughout a wide pH range, which is consistent with the experimental data.

Temperature plays a crucial role in the electrocatalytic process. To understand its impact on OTC degradation, experiments were conducted as shown in Fig. 4(i). The degradation efficiency of OTC increased with temperature, reaching 89.56 %, 90.04 %, 90.50 %, and 89.06 % at temperatures from 25 °C to 35 °C, respectively. However, when the temperature rose from 35 °C to 40 °C, the degradation efficiency unexpectedly decreased to 89.01 %. This is because higher temperatures typically enhance OTC elimination by increasing molecular collisions. Conversely, exceeding an optimal temperature threshold led to more side reactions, which in turn reduced current efficiency and ∙OH consumption. These results indicate that achieving a higher degradation rate is feasible at the optimal reaction temperature. Notably, the degradation efficiency of oxytetracycline hydrochloride over 60 min remained relatively consistent within the temperature range of 25 °C to 35 °C, underscoring that the Pd-BCQD/Ti electrode offers effective OTC degradation across a broad temperature spectrum.

In this experiment, the cathode and anode can work in tandem to deteriorate the OTC because they are in the same system. On the anode surface, active chlorine species may undergo direct, indirect, or mixed oxidation. Direct electron transport between the active layer on the electrode surface and the OTC adsorbed on the anode surface allows for direct oxidation (Eq. (6). The primary process in indirect oxidation is the oxidation of water, which results in non-homogeneous ·OH (Eq. (1). The amount of ·OH produced at the composite electrode during the electrocatalytic process was measured using fluorescence spectroscopy. When demonstrated in Fig. 5 (a), a progressive increase in the ·OH content was indicated by the fluorescence intensity of 7-hydroxycoumarin when the electrocatalysis period was extended up to 60 min. According to the data, OTC underwent oxidative degradation via the use of ·OH, which was produced during the electrocatalytic reaction (Eq. (7). Fig. 5(b) shows the zone analysis of the oxygen reaction and chlorine precipitation process by performing linear voltammetric scans in different electrolyte solutions. As shown in Fig. 5(b), the oxygen evolution reaction in Na2SO4 solution occurs at the anode surface, with an approximate oxygen evolution potential of 1.20 V vs. SCE. The onset potential progressively drops as the concentration of chloride ions rises, as the inset in Fig. 5(b) illustrates. This is because high concentrations of chloride ions have the ability to both efficiently prevent oxygen evolution and encourage the synthesis of active chlorine intermediates. Consequently, the surface of the electrocatalytic anode in the NaCl system may experience the subsequent chemical processes (Eqs. (8)–(10)). The study's reaction solution had a pH of 4.5, hence the majority of the active chlorine species that were produced were HClO. In order to elucidate the role of ·OH and H* on OTC degradation, isopropyl alcohol (IPA) was chosen to quench ·OH, and tertiary butyl alcohol (TBA) was used to quench ·OH and H* for free radical trapping experiments. In this study, IPA at a concentration of 1 M was added to the OTC solution to quench the ·OH generated during the reaction, as shown in Fig. 5(c), when IPA was added, the degradation rate of OTC decreased to 75.54 %, which was reduced by 14.96 %, and this result can indirectly prove that ·OH plays an important role in the degradation of OTC in the electrocatalytic system. Furthermore, the presence of IPA did not totally stop OTC from degrading; around 75 % of OTC could still be broken down 60 min after IPA was added, suggesting that direct oxidation at the anode still plays a role in OTC degradation.

On the cathode surface, in addition to the anode, direct reduction, indirect reduction, and H2O2 oxidation take place. By direct electron transfer, direct reduction is accomplished (Eq. (11), Fig. 5(e) step ①). However, after the addition of TBA at a concentration of 1 M during the degradation process, the OTC degradation efficiency decreased to 73.28 %, which was 2.26 % lower than that after the addition of IPA, proving that H* also contributes to the whole system, i.e., indirectly reducing (Eqs. (10)–(12), Fig. 5(e), steps ② and ③). In this study, the Pd-BCQDs/Ti electrode was used as the cathode, in which Pd, as an excellent hydrogen adsorption metal, can effectively adsorb and desorb hydrogen during the electrocatalytic process. And due to the good electrical conductivity of Pd, when it is combined with carbon quantum dots, the overall conductivity of the composite can be significantly improved, thus promoting the electron transfer in the electrocatalytic process. In addition, the introduction of Pd can accelerate the electrocatalytic degradation process by providing more active sites to effectively reduce the activation energy of the reaction. These active sites form H* and ·OH by adsorbing and dissociating water molecules. On the other hand, the interlayer of this electrode employs B-CQDs. The charge distribution inside the carbon quantum dots can be effectively adjusted due to the introduction of B, which can help to improve the charge transfer efficiency during the electrocatalytic degradation process, and the doping of B in carbon quantum dots improves the electrochemical stability of the material while enhancing its electrical conductivity by improving the electronic structure of the carbon quantum dots to enhance their electrical conductivity. In addition, B doping can optimise the surface properties of the material, for example by increasing the hydrophilicity of the surface, which helps to improve the contact efficiency of the catalyst with the reaction medium. Functional groups such as hydroxyl or carboxyl groups on the surface of carbon quantum dots can act as sites for the formation of H* and ·OH. The presence of these functional groups can catalyze the decomposition of water to produce these key reaction intermediates. Notably, the higher reactivity in the boron doped region can effectively catalyze the water decomposition reaction and promote the generation of H* and ·OH. After combining Pd with B-CQDs, the advantages of Pd in catalyzing hydrogenation and dehydrogenation reactions combined with the high electrocatalytic activity of B-CQDs can achieve an efficient co-catalytic effect in electrocatalytic degradation of OTCs, which together promote the formation of H* and ·OH. The concentration of H2O2 in the reaction solution can be detected using the iodometric method to demonstrate the contribution of H2O2 produced by oxygen reduction on the cathode to the OTC degradation (Eq. (13), Fig. 5(e) step ④). As the degradation period was extended, as seen in Fig. 5(d), the concentration of H2O2 in the solution progressively rose to 0.228 mmol/L. The findings showed that H2O2 might produce ·OH in this experiment, which would cause OTC to degrade (Eq. (16) and Fig. 5(e) step ⑤).

Several OTC degradation intermediates were detected by LC-MS, and possible OTC degradation pathways were proposed based on the identified intermediates and previous related reports (Fig. 6 ). The three possible degradation pathways were as follows: (1) OTC double bond breakage and hydroxylation to give P1 (m/z = 478). The dimethylamino group in P1 was oxidized, and one hydroxyl group was removed to give P2 (m/z = 417). P2 underwent ring-opening cleavage and further disproportionation to give P3 (m/z = 364), P4 (m/z = 277) and P5 (m/z = 114). (2) OTC removed one methyl group of the dimethylamino group to obtain P6 (m/z = 430), which was attacked by ·OH, and after CC bond breaking and ring-opening cleavage, progressively obtained P7 (m/z = 318) and P8 (m/z = 246). The keto group of P8 was reduced to a hydroxyl group and ring-opened to obtain P9 (m/z = 116). (3) Stepwise demethylation and hydroxylation of the dimethylamino group on the OTC molecule resulted in P10 (m/z = 464) and P11 (m/z = 418), and P11 was dehydrated and the CC bond was broken to produce P12 (m/z = 359), which was further dehydrated and ring-opened to produce P13 (m/z = 274), and then demethylated by P13 and the CC bond was broken to produce P14 (m/z = 162), and P14 was ring-opened to obtain P14 (m/z = 162). P14 was further dehydrated and ring-opened to produce P15 (m/z = 91). With the extension of the reaction time, P5, P9, and P15 were finally mineralized into small molecules such as H2O and CO2 to achieve efficient degradation of OTC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Toxicity analysis of OTC and its degradation products
ce:section.ce:para: The ECOSAR program was utilized to simulate the ecotoxicity of OTC and 15 degradation products. Fig. 7 (a) displays the outcomes of acute and chronic toxicity assessments, while Table S1 lists the Globally Harmonized System (GHS) toxicity classification levels. In Fig. 7(a), the color spectrum from red to blue depicts a decreasing gradient of toxicity. According to the toxicity assessment, OTC poses both acute and chronic risks to daphnia and green algae but is not acutely hazardous to fish. Following the electrocatalytic degradation of OTC using Pd-BCQD/Ti electrodes, there was a notable reduction in the compounds' toxicity. Specifically, degradation intermediates P6, P8, and P14 exhibited acute toxicity to fish. However, with an extended reaction time, these intermediates could be transformed into the non-toxic compounds P7, P9, and P15. The acute toxicity of the 15 degradation products to daphnia was found to be either similar to or less than that of OTC. Regarding green algae, P6 was more acutely toxic than OTC, but it too could be converted into the non-toxic P7. All degradation products demonstrated chronic toxicity levels that were either equivalent to or lower than that of OTC. Therefore, employing Pd-BCQD/Ti electrodes for OTC degradation emerges as a safe and environmentally friendly approach.

As indicated in Fig. 7(b–d) and Table S2, the developmental toxicity, mutagenicity, and bioaccumulation factors (BCFs) of OTC and its degradation intermediates were assessed using predictions made by the toxicity evaluation software (TEST) based on quantitative constitutive relationships (QSARs). The products of Pd-BCQD/Ti's electrocatalytic degradation, P3 and P4, are more toxic than the OTC in Fig. 7(b), but as the reaction progresses, P4 and P13 can be further transformed to P5 and P14, which have lower toxicity. Slightly less than that of the parent OTC, Fig. 7(c) shows that the mutagenicity values of P5, P9, P13, P14, and P15 in the three degradation routes were decreased to “negative”. And the remaining intermediates were still classified as “positive”. The BFCs of P3, P4, and P13 were not accessible for the test prediction technique due to the lack of relevant experimental data, and the BFCs of OTC and its degradation intermediates are displayed in Fig. 7(d). With P1, P5, and P14 having greater BFCs than those of the parent OTCs, the majority of these intermediates greatly decreased the bioaccumulation factors. However, P1 and P14 might be further converted to P2 and P15 through the reaction. It is worth noting that P5 has a high bioaccumulation factor and should be monitored in real time to prevent the overproduction of P5, which is harmful to the environment.

Investigating the reusability of the test electrode provides insights into its stability. After undergoing 60 min of electrocatalytic degradation using a 20 mg/L OTC solution, the electrode was removed, thoroughly cleansed with ultrapure water, and dried. Subsequently, it was reused ten additional times for OTC degradation; the results are shown in Fig. 8 (a). Notably, over ten consistent reuses of the electrode, the OTC degradation efficiency slightly decreased from 90.50 % to 89.88 %. Moreover, after a 60-minute degradation cycle, the XRD spectra of the Pd-BCQD/Ti electrode remained largely consistent with the original spectra (Fig. 8b). These findings suggest that the composite electrode, loaded with Pd particles and BCQD, demonstrates stable electrocatalytic performance.

To assess the Pd-BCQD/Ti composite electrode's general applicability for antibiotic pollutants, degradation tests were carried out on model pollutants, such as tetracycline hydrochloride (TC), norfloxacin (NOR), doxycycline hydrochloride (DOX), sulfamethoxazole (SMX), and tylosin (TYL). As seen in Fig. 8(c), the composite electrode's tetracycline hydrochloride removal efficiency (89.01 %) was second only to the OTC degradation rate. In contrast, the antibiotics had the following respective degradation rates: η(TYL) = 83.14 %, η(NOR) = 73.37 %, η(DOX) = 66.76 %, and η(SMX) = 43.17 %. The outcomes demonstrated that additional antimicrobial contaminants could also be effectively degraded by the Pd-BCQD/Ti electrode. The compositional complexity of actual water is larger than that of ultrapure water, which could have an impact on the electrode materials' catalytic performance. As a result, OTC degradation tests were carried out in a range of water conditions to demonstrate the viability of the Pd-BCQD/Ti electrode. The OTC degradation rates in tap water and Songhua River water were 88.01 % and 87.59 %, respectively, as shown in Fig. 8(d). In ultrapure water, OTC degraded with an efficiency of 90.50 %; however, OTC breakdown in real water bodies was somewhat lower. The Pd-BCQD/Ti electrode has a wide range of applications in actual OTC wastewater treatment, as evidenced by the OTC degradation rates of over 87 % in both genuine water bodies.

Table 2
[48–51] compares the Pd-BCQD/Ti electrode's ability to eliminate OTC with that of other recognized catalysts. When it came to breaking down OTC quickly, the Pd-BCQD/Ti electrode performed remarkably better than the catalysts that had been previously reported. The findings indicate that there is a great deal of promise for the Pd-BCQD/Ti electrode developed by this study to remove antibiotic pollutants from water.

This work generated Pd-BCQD/Ti bifunctional cathode catalysts and suggested a coupling scheme. Through a synergistic reaction mechanism, the combined effects of ·OH oxidation and H* reduction were used to drive efficient degrading performance. The electrode's exceptional catalytic activity was proven by electrochemical characterization. In the meantime, the Pd-BCQD/Ti electrode's preparation parameters and testing setup were refined. The results demonstrated that, in ideal circumstances, OTC elimination efficiency may reach 90.50 %. After loading Pd and BCQD, the electrode energy consumption decreased by 0.026 kWh/m3 while the current efficiency increased by 0.228 %. In addition, the electrode has attractive stability and universality. After ten cycles of degradation experiments, the removal rate still reached 89.88 %, and it had a good degradation effect on different antibiotics. In the electrocatalytic system of Pd-BCQD/Ti electrodes, mechanistic study demonstrated that the cathode and anode worked in concert, and the cathode produced H2O2 up to a concentration of 0.228 mmol/L. H*, ·OH, and HClO generation were associated with the effective OTC degradation. Lastly, to prove the OTC degradation intermediates' environmentally beneficial qualities, a toxicity assessment was done. This study offers a fresh viewpoint on how to build redox synergistic systems to improve electrocatalytic performance.
